To the fourth: The delay (التراخي) indicated by the word "then" (ثم), just as it suits the meaning of nurturing and domestication as they mentioned, also suits the meaning of cutting, dispersing the parts, and placing them on the mountains, as is apparent.

To the fifth: The problem is reversed upon them. For what they mentioned is that Allah only clarified the modality of revival to Abraham through theoretical, scientific explanation (البيان العلمي النظري) rather than through witnessing (الشهودي). Thus, the objection returns to them that it would then have been appropriate to conclude the verse with the attribute of power (القدرة) rather than the attributes of might and wisdom. You have already learned from what we presented that the most appropriate, according to the meaning of the verse we explained, is the conclusion with the two names: the All-mighty, the All-wise, as in the verse.

From what we have mentioned, the invalidity of what some other commentators have stated also becomes apparent: that the intention of the question in the verse is only the request for witnessing the modality of revival in the sense of how the parts accept the form of life.

He said, in summary: that the question was not in a religious matter—Allah forbid—but it was a question about the modality of revival to encompass knowledge of it; and encompassing the form of the modality of revival is not a condition for faith (الايمان). Thus, Abraham (peace be upon him) requested a knowledge upon which faith does not depend. This is indicated by the question coming in the form of "how" (كيف), and its subject is the inquiry about the state (الحال). A parallel to this is when one says: "How does Zayd judge among the people?" for he does not doubt that he judges among them, but he asks about the modality of his judgment whose occurrence is known. Had he been asking about the occurrence of that, he would have said: "Does Zayd judge among the people?" The confirmation—namely His saying: "Do you not believe?"—only came after it because that form, although used apparently to ask about modality as you have learned, may also be used to express the seeking of proof of inability (الاستعجاز), as when a claimant claims that he can carry a certain weight and you know his inability to carry it, so you say to him: "Show me how you carry this," intending that you are unable to carry it. When Allah the Glorified knew Abraham's (peace be upon him) innocence of hovering around this sanctuary, He wanted to make him speak in the answer with that which would repel that linguistic possibility in the first expression—since his faith was sincere—with an expression that explicitly states it such that everyone who hears it understands it with an understanding in which no doubt interferes. The meaning of tranquility (الطمانينة) then is the soul's stillness from wandering among the possible modalities of revival through the appearance of the witnessed form; and the non-attainment of this tranquility before does not contradict the attainment of faith in the power to revive in the most perfect manner. Seeing the modality did not add anything to the faith required of him; it only provided a matter that faith in it is not obligatory.

Then he said after a long discourse: The verse indicates the merit of Abraham (peace be upon him) in that Allah the Glorified showed him what he asked for immediately in the easiest of ways, while He showed Uzayr what He showed him after He caused him to die for a hundred years.

By reflecting on the verse and considering the explanation we have provided, you will recognize the collapse of what he mentioned. For the question occurred regarding the modality of *His* revival, the Exalted, not about the modality of the parts' acceptance of life, secondly; for it was said: "how You revive" (تُحيي) with the *damma* on the *ta*, not with the *fatha*. Furthermore, the execution of the matter by Abraham's (peace be upon him) hand indicates that. Had the question been about the modality of acceptance, it would have sufficed to show some of the dead whom Allah revives, as in the story of the one who passed by the ruined town in the previous verse where the Exalted said: "And look at the bones, how We raise them, then We clothe them with flesh," and there would have been no need for the revival to be carried out by Abraham's hand. This is what we alluded to just now: that they measure the souls of the prophets in their reception of divine gnosis (المعارف الالهية) and their being the source of miraculous matters by their own ordinary souls. This results, for example, in there being no difference between life being formed by Abraham's hand and its being formed externally with respect to Abraham's state. This is a matter that does not occur to the mind of one who searches for realities and is expert in them; but these people, due to their neglect of the matter of realities, fell into the corruption they fell into, and the more they delved into the research, the further they moved from the truth. Do you not see that he interpreted tranquility as the removal of thoughts regarding the possible forms in the formation and the conceived shapes, while this intellectual hesitation is among the vanities that have no path to the sanctuary of one like Abraham (peace be upon him)? Moreover, the answer narrated in the verse did not provide anything in that regard; for Abraham (peace be upon him) said: "how You revive the dead?" using the term "the dead" (الموتى) generally, intending the human dead or what is more general than that, yet Allah the Glorified only showed him the formation of life in four birds.

Then he mentioned the merit of Abraham (peace be upon him) over Uzayr (intending the protagonist of the story in the previous verse) by what he mentioned, thus taking the story in the two verses as being of one type—namely the question about modality which he interpreted as he did, and the answer to it. Thus, the meaning of the two verses together became confused for him, even though both verses—despite the gems of explanation and subtleties of meaning they contain—are foreign to modality in the sense he mentioned. All of this is clear by referring to what has passed regarding them.

Furthermore, what is appropriate for the clarification of modality is concluding the verse with the attribute of power (القدرة), not with the attributes of might and wisdom, as in the Exalted's saying: **وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ أَنَّكَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ خَاشِعَةً فَإِذَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا الْمَاءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ إِنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — "And among His signs is that you see the earth desolate, but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells. Indeed He who revives it is the reviver of the dead. Indeed He has power over all things" (41:39). The verse, as you see, is in the context of clarifying the modality and has been concluded with the attribute of absolute power. Similar to it is the Exalted's saying: **وَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَعْيَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَىٰ أَن يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتَىٰ بَلَىٰ إِنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — "Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not exhausted by their creation, is able to revive the dead? Yes, indeed He has power over all things" (46:33). In it also is a clarification of the modality by showing examples, then the speech is concluded with the attribute of power.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "He said, 'Do you not believe?' He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.'" *Bala* (بلى) is a word used to rebut a negation, and therefore the negation is turned into an affirmation by it, as in the Exalted's saying: **أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ** — "‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes’" (7:172). Had they said *na'am* (نعم), it would have been disbelief. Tranquility (الطمانينة) and being at rest (الاطمينان) is the soul's stillness after its agitation and disturbance. It is derived from their saying: "the land is tranquil" (اطمأنت الارض) and "a tranquil land" (ارض مطمئنة) when it has a depression in which water settles when it flows to it and a stone when it descends to it.

The Exalted said: "Do you not believe?" (أولم تؤمن) and did not say: "Do you not believe?" (ألم تؤمن) to signal that there is a place for the question and request, but it should not be accompanied by a lack of faith in the revival. If it were said: "Do you not believe?" (ألم تؤمن), it would indicate that the speaker received the question as arising from a lack of faith, so it would be a reproach and a deterrent from such a question. This is because the *waw* (و) is for conjunction, so the interrogation with it was an inquiry into whether this question is accompanied by a lack of faith, not an inquiry into the reason for the question such that it would result in reproach and deterrence.

Faith (الايمان) is absolute in His speech, the Exalted, and in it is an indication that faith in Allah the Glorified is not realized with doubt in the matter of revival and resurrection. This is not contradicted by the specific instance being about revival, because the instance does not necessitate the specification of the word's generality nor the restriction of its absolute sense.

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted, narrating from him (peace be upon him): "that my heart may be at rest," is absolute, indicating that his (peace be upon him) objective from this question was the attainment of absolute tranquility and the cutting off of the sources of every heart-felt thought and their roots. For the estimation (الوهم) in its particular perceptions and its judgments, since it is devoted to the door of the senses and most of its judgments and affirmations are in the percepts it receives through the senses, it shrinks from complying with what the intellect (العقل) affirms, even if the soul is believing and certain of it. This is like the true universal intellectual judgments (الاحكام الكلية العقلية) regarding matters outside of matter and hidden from the senses; for it [the estimation] refuses to accept them even if it submits to the premises that produce them, so it brings to mind judgments that contradict them, then it stirs the psychological states appropriate to its refusal, so it becomes strong and supported thereby in its opposing influence, even if the soul, from the side of its intellect, is certain of the judgment and believes in the matter—it does not harm it except as an annoyance. Just as one who spends the night in a dark house in which there is a dead body knows that the dead person is an inanimate object without perception or will, so it does not harm anything; yet the estimation refuses this result and demands from the imagination (المتخيلة) to depict for the soul terrifying and lonely images of the dead person's matter, then the attribute of fear is stirred and dominates the soul, and it may reach a point where the intellect vanishes or the soul departs.

It has thus become clear that the existence of thoughts (الخطورات) contrary to certain beliefs (العقائد اليقينية) does not always contradict faith and affirmation, except that they annoy the soul and strip stillness and قرار from it. The existence of these thoughts is not removed except by sense or witnessing. For this reason, it was said: "Indeed, for direct observation (المعاينة) there is an effect that is not found with knowledge." Allah the Exalted informed Moses at the appointed time of his people's straying through the worship of the calf, yet that did not necessitate the appearance of his anger until he came to them and witnessed them and observed their matter directly; then he became angry, threw down the tablets, and took his brother by the head, pulling him toward himself.

It has become clear from here and from what preceded that Abraham (peace be upon him) was not asking for witnessing through the sense that relates to the material parts' acceptance of life after losing it; rather, he was only asking to witness the action of Allah the Glorified and His command in reviving the dead. This is not something sensed, even if it is inseparable from the sensed matter which is the material parts' acceptance of life through gathering and taking the form of a living being. Thus, he (peace be upon him) was only asking for the "truth of certitude" (حق اليقين).

**His saying, the Exalted:** "He said, 'Then take four birds and incline them (فصرهن) to you, then place on every mountain a part of them, then call them; they will come to you in haste.'" *Surhunna* (صرهن) with a *damma* on the *sad* according to one of the two readings is from *sara yasuru* when he cuts or inclines; or with a *kasra* on the *sad* according to the other reading from *sara yasiru* with one of the two meanings. The context of the speech indicates the intention of the meaning of cutting, and its being made transitive with "to" (إلى) indicates the implication of the meaning of inclining. So the meaning is: cut them while inclining them to you, or incline them to you while cutting them, depending on the disagreement regarding the implication in terms of estimation.

In any case, His saying, the Exalted: "Take four birds..." etc., is an answer to what Abraham (peace be upon him) asked by his saying: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead." It is known that the answer must correspond to the question; the eloquence of speech and the wisdom of the speaker prevent the speech from containing what is vain and redundant, from whose existence no benefit returns to the intended purpose of the speech—especially the Quran, which is the best speech delivered by the best speaker to the best attentive listener. The story is not of that simplicity that appears from it at first glance. Had it been so, the answer would have been completed by the revival of any dead thing in any manner, and the excess over that would have been vain and dispensable. But it is not so. Constraints and additional specificities have been taken in it beyond the original meaning: it was required that what was to be revived be a bird, that it be alive, that it be of the number four, that it be killed, mixed, and its parts blended, that the mixed parts be dispersed into portions, then each portion be placed in a location distant from the other like the peak of this mountain and that mountain, and that the revival be by Abraham's hand (the questioner himself) through his calling them, and that they all gather with him.

These, as you see, are additional specificities in the story that are undoubtedly involved in the meaning intended to be conveyed. They have mentioned for them aspects of subtleties that only increase the researcher's wonder (the correctness of what we mentioned is known by referring to the detailed commentaries).

In any case, these specificities must be connected to the question. What is found in the question—which is his saying: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead"—are two matters.

**One of them:** What is contained in His saying "You revive" (تحيي), which is that the request was to witness the revival insofar as it is an attribute of Allah the Glorified, not insofar as it is an attribute of the parts of matter carrying life.

**The second of them:** What is contained in the word "the dead" (الموتى) of the meaning of plurality, for it is an additional specificity.

**As for the first:** There is connected to it in the answer the execution of this matter by Abraham's own hand where He says: "Then take," "and incline them," "then place," in the imperative form, and He says: "then call them; they will come to you," for the Exalted made their coming in haste—which is life—connected to and branching from the call. This call is the cause from which the life of what was intended to be revived overflows. There is no revival except by Allah's command; thus, Abraham's calling them by Allah's command was connected with a certain connection to Allah's command from which the life of the living trickles. At that point, Abraham witnessed it and saw the modality of the overflow of the command with life. Had Abraham's calling them not been connected to Allah's command—which is to say to a thing He willed: "Be," and it is—like our words which are not connected except by imagination, he would also have been like us: if we said to a thing "Be," it would not be. For there is no haphazard influence in existence.

**As for the second:** His saying "how You revive the dead" indicates that the plurality of the dead and their multiplicity has an involvement in the question. This is only because bodies, by their death, the dispersal of their parts, the change of their forms, and the transformation of their states, lose the state of distinction and connection that was between them, so they become lost in the darkness of annihilation and ruin, and become like forgotten tales of which there is no news in reality or mind; so how does the reviving power encompass them when there is nothing encompassed in reality?

This is what Pharaoh raised to Moses (peace be upon him), and Moses answered him with knowledge as Allah narrated by His saying: **قَالَ فَمَا بَالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولَىٰ قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّي فِي كِتَابٍ لَّا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلَا يَنسَى** — "He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets’" (20:51-52).

In summary, Allah answered him by commanding him to take four birds (and perhaps the choice of birds was because this act is easier and takes less time in them) so he could witness their life and see the difference of their individuals and forms, and know them with a complete knowledge first; then kill them and mix their parts with a fine mixing, then make that into portions, and place each portion of them on a mountain so that distinction and identification are lost, and knowledge vanishes; then call them, and they will come to him in haste. For he witnesses then that distinction and taking the form of life—all of that is following the call which relates to their souls (أنفسها), i.e., that their bodies follow their souls and not the reverse. For the body is a branch following the spirit (الروح), not the reverse; rather, the relationship of the body to the spirit is, in a way, the relationship of the shadow to the object. When the object exists, its existence is followed by the existence of the shadow, and to whatever state the object or its parts transform, the shadow follows it in that, until if it is annihilated, it follows it in annihilation. When Allah the Glorified brings a living being into existence, or returns life to parts previously possessed of life, His bringing into existence only relates to the spirit possessing life first, then the parts of matter follow it through links preserved with Allah the Glorified which we do not encompass in knowledge; thus the body is determined by the determination of the spirit without separation or obstacle. By this, His saying, the Exalted, signals: "then call them; they will come to you in haste (سعياً)," i.e., speeding and hurrying.

This is what is benefited from the Exalted's saying: **وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be created anew?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). Some discussion of the verse has already passed in the research on the immateriality of the soul (تجرد النفس), and the detailed discussion will come in its place, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: "Then take four birds," he was only commanded that so he would know them and thus not doubt them when life is returned to them nor deny them, and so he would see what they are upon of difference and distinction first and their disappearance second. His saying: "and incline them to you, then place on every mountain a part of them," i.e., slaughter them, scatter their parts, and mix them, then disperse them on the mountains present there so that the parts become distant while being indistinct. This is among the evidences that the story only occurred after Abraham's migration from the land of Babylon to Syria, for the land of Babylon has no mountains. His saying: "then call them," i.e., call the birds: "O peacock," "O so-and-so," and "O so-and-so." This can be benefited, in addition to the indication of the pronoun "them" (هن) referring to the birds, from His saying: "call them" (ادعهن); for if the call were for the parts of the birds rather than their souls, it would have been more appropriate to say: "then hail them" (نادهن), for they were on mountains distant from his (peace be upon him) position, and the word used specifically for the distant is "hailing" (النداء) rather than "calling" (الدعاء). His saying: "they will come to you in haste," i.e., they became embodied and were described with coming and speeding to you.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "And know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise," i.e., All-mighty (عزيز) who loses nothing by its departure from Him, All-wise (حكيم) who does nothing except through its appropriate path; thus He brings bodies into existence by summoning the spirits and bringing them into existence, not the reverse.

In His saying, the Exalted: "And know that..." rather than saying "Indeed Allah is...", there is an indication that the heart-felt thought from whose side Abraham was asking his Lord for witnessing so that his heart might be at rest was returning to the reality of the meaning of the two names: the All-mighty, the All-wise. Thus, Allah the Glorified provided him with this answer the knowledge of their reality.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding the Exalted's saying: "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord..." the verse: al-Tayalisi and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) who said: "The one who argued with Abraham about his Lord was Nimrod ibn Canaan."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*: Abu Ali al-Tabarsi said: There is disagreement regarding the time of this argument. It was said it was at the breaking of the idols before he was cast into the fire, narrated from Muqatil; and it was said it was after he was cast into the fire and it was made for him cool and safe, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

**I say:** Although the verse does not address whether it was before or after, consideration supports its being after the casting into the fire. For his stories mentioned in the Quran at the beginning of his affair—from his arguing with his father and his people and his breaking the idols—convey that the first time Abraham (peace be upon him) met Nimrod was when his case was raised to him in the matter of breaking the idols as a criminal among them. So he judged him to be burned, and the judgment upon him for his crime occupied them from speaking to him about the matter of his Lord: is He Allah or Nimrod? Had Nimrod argued with him then, he would have argued with him about the matter of Allah and the matter of the idols, not the matter of Allah and the matter of himself!

In several narrations narrated by both the Sunnis and the Shi'a regarding the Exalted's saying: "Or him who passed by a town which had fallen on its trellises..." the verse: that the protagonist of the story was Jeremiah the Prophet; and in several of them: that it was Uzayr. However, they are isolated reports (آحاد) not mandatory to accept, and in their chains of transmission (أسانيد) there is some weakness, and they have no witness from the apparent meaning of the verses. The story is not mentioned in the Torah, and what is in the narrations of the story is long and contains some variation, but it is outside our purpose; whoever wants it should refer to its sources.

In *al-Ma'ani*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the Exalted's saying: "And when Abraham said, 'My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead...'" the verse: in a hadith, he (peace be upon him) said: "This is an allegorical verse (آية متشابهة), and its meaning is that he asked about the modality (الكيفية), and modality is from the action of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. Whenever the scholar does not know it, no blame attaches to him, nor does any deficiency occur in his monotheism (توحيده)." The hadith.

**I say:** The meaning of the hadith has become clear from what has passed.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Ali ibn Asbat: that Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah: "He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest'": Was there doubt in his heart? He said: "No, but he wanted an increase from Allah." The hadith.

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Kafi* from al-Sadiq and from the Righteous Servant (peace be upon them both), and its explanation has passed.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Abu Ayyub, from Abu Basir, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Abraham looked at a carcass on the seashore being eaten by the beasts of the sea, then the beasts would leap upon one another and eat each other. Abraham wondered and said: 'O Lord! Show me how You revive the dead?' Allah said: 'Do you not believe?' He said: 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.' He said: 'Then take four birds and incline them to you, then place on every mountain a part of them, then call them; they will come to you in haste, and know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise.' So Abraham took the peacock, the rooster, the pigeon, and the crow. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Incline them to you,' meaning cut them, then mix their flesh and disperse them on ten mountains. Then he called them and said: 'Come to life by the permission of Allah.' So they began to gather and join, flesh to flesh and bone to bone, until each bird came to its head, and they flew to Abraham. At that point, Abraham said: 'Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise.'"

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his *Tafsir* from Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and it was narrated through Sunni paths from Ibn Abbas.

His saying: "Abraham looked at a carcass..." until his saying "so he said: O Lord! Show me..." etc., is an explanation of the ambiguity that led him to the question, which is the dispersal of the body's parts after death—a dispersal leading to their change and transition to different places and diverse states such that nothing of the original remains.

**If you say:** The apparent meaning of the narration is that the ambiguity was the "problem of the eater and the eaten" (شبهة الآكل والمأكول), as it included the leaping of some and the eating of some by others, then branched Abraham's wonder and question upon that.

**I say:** The ambiguity is two ambiguities: **one of them** is the dispersal of the body's parts and the annihilation of their original forms and accidents, and in summary, their non-persistence such that they could be distinguished and life could be composed in them; **and the second of them** is the parts of some animals becoming part of the body of another, leading to the impossibility of reviving both animals with their complete bodies together, because the premise is that a part of the body of one of them is identically a part of the body of the other; so if each one of them were returned complete, the other would remain deficient and not accept restoration. This is the problem of the eater and the eaten.

What Allah the Glorified answered with—which is the body's following of the spirit—even if it is sufficient to repel both ambiguities together, yet what Abraham was commanded with according to what the verse narrates does not contain the material for the problem of the eater and the eaten, which is the eating of some animals by others; rather, it only contains the dispersal of parts, their mixing, and the change of their forms and states. This is the material for the first ambiguity. Thus, the verse only addresses its repulsion, even if both ambiguities share in being repelled by what was answered in the verse as previously mentioned. What the narration contained regarding the eating of some by others is not intended in the interpretation of the verse.

His saying (peace be upon him): "So Abraham took the peacock, the rooster, the pigeon, and the crow." In some narrations, the birds were the vulture, the duck, the peacock, and the rooster; narrated by al-Saduq in *al-Uyun* from al-Rida (peace be upon him) and quoted from Mujahid, Ibn Jurayj, Ata, and Ibn Zayd. In some of them: they were the hoopoe, the shrike, the peacock, and the crow; narrated by al-Ayyashi from Salih ibn Sahl from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). In some of them: they were the ostrich, the peacock, the goose, and the rooster; narrated by al-Ayyashi from Ma'ruf ibn Kharrabudh from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) and quoted from Ibn Abbas. It was also narrated through Sunni paths from Ibn Abbas: they were the crane, the peacock, the rooster, and the pigeon. That which all narrations and opinions share is: the peacock.

His saying (peace be upon him): "And disperse them on ten mountains." The mountains being ten is what the reports transmitted from the Imams of the People of the House (أئمة اهل البيت) agreed upon. It was said they were four, and it was said seven.

In *al-Uyun*, with a chain of transmission from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida Ali ibn Musa was with him. Al-Ma'mun said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah! Is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He said: 'Yes.' So he asked him about verses from the Quran. Among what he asked him was that he said: 'Then inform me about the saying of Allah: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead. He said, 'Do you not believe?' He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.'"' Al-Rida said: 'Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted, had revealed to Abraham: "I am taking from My servants a friend (خليلا); if he asks Me to revive the dead, I will answer him." It occurred in Abraham's heart that he was that friend, so he said: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead?" He said: "Do you not believe?" He said: "Yes, but that my heart may be at rest regarding the friendship (الخلة)."'" The hadith.

**I say:** A discussion regarding Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm and this narration which he narrated from al-Rida (peace be upon him) has already preceded in the reports of Adam's Garden, so refer back to it.

Know that the narration is not devoid of some indication that the station of friendship (مقام الخلة) necessitates the answering of prayer (استجابة الدعاء). The wording supports this, for friendship (الخلة) is need (الحاجة), and the friend (الخليل) is only called a friend because when friendship is complete, the friend raises his needs to his friend, and there is no meaning to raising them without sufficiency and fulfillment.
